item.title: I almost ditched Claude over its brutal rate limits, but then it nailed something no other AI could
item.itunes:title: I almost ditched Claude over its brutal rate limits, but then it nailed something no other AI could
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
After hearing good things about Anthropic's Claude AI, I wanted to give the chatbot a try for myself. I'm usually skeptical of LLM tools because of their habit of hallucinating, so I wanted to see if Claude really offered a different experience.

While I was aware that Claude had limits for users on its free and paid plans, I didn't realize just how restrictive they could be. This meant that I almost gave up on using the tool entirely.

I tested Claude’s new interactive visuals, and they’re changing how I explain things

Most LLMs suffer with visualisation, Claude doesn't

2
By  Abhinav Raj

My first session with Claude was extremely limited

I started using the tool as Anthropic ramped up peak-usage limits

Limits for free users of AI tools are nothing new, but I didn't realize how restrictive Claude would be in comparison to others. Within two prompts, I had reached my free message limit for five hours — meaning that I would need to upgrade to a paid plan or wait until five hours elapsed if I wanted to continue.

I was testing different AI tools to help me with my taxes, and purposefully stuck to free plans so that I could compare the models available to most casual users. However, Claude was the only tool in which I encountered usage limits. When my free message quota refreshed, it was used again within two prompts.

This meant that what took 30 minutes to an hour in other tools took multiple days in Claude. I realized that I wasn't the only one experiencing this issue, as users had taken to the Claude subreddit to complain about how quickly session limits were taking effect.

Thariq Shihipar, a member of the technical staff at Anthropic, posted on X that five-hour session limits had been adjusted during peak hours. This meant that free, Pro, and Max users would move through their session limits faster from 5am to 11am PT. According to Shihipar, the change was made to manage the growing demand for Claude.

I was not alone in my frustration. And it made me wonder whether it was worth bothering with the tool at all.

Claude's quality meant that I persisted

I also tweaked my usage behavior

I eventually completed my test of Claude during off-peak hours, where I didn't encounter the annoying limits anymore. I was able to finish the prompts I had prepared, as well as add some follow-up prompts.

Notably, in my test on my tax spreadsheet, Claude was the only tool that managed to get my income correct. This immediately made me pause and reconsider my plan to ditch the tool as soon as I was done with my test. As a result, I decided to test it for a few other things.

In addition to the quality of its responses, other factors won me over. When it comes to visualizing data and creating charts, spreadsheets, and checklists, Claude goes the extra mile. For example, its ability to color-code my spreadsheets is helpful when dealing with a lot of data. When it comes to its responsive visualizations, I can download them as HTML files so that I can view them in my browser whenever I need them.

I also enjoy its Projects feature, which is something that a tool like Gemini currently lacks. Projects are also useful for burning through usage limits more slowly, since Claude caches the data in your uploaded files.

When I used Claude to help me brainstorm more articles based on successful ones I'd done in the past, I found that its results were particularly impressive.

As a result, I'm sticking with the tool — though I'm saving my more intensive tasks for off-peak hours.

The backlash is understandable though

Especially for paying users

While I'm sticking with Claude, this doesn't mean I don't understand the frustration that users are feeling. I am a freelancer and I work from home, so I am able to simply shift my hours around to use Claude during off-peak periods. This isn't the case for many people.

I think I'd also be more frustrated if I was a paying user. I am entertaining the idea of a Claude Pro subscription, but I think peak time usage limits would sting a lot more if I was being told when I could effectively use a product I was paying for.

I also don't have a particularly intensive workflow — I mostly use AI tools for brainstorming. However, peak time usage limits can be detrimental to heavier workflows, especially with Claude's propensity to provide comprehensive answers. This makes it more difficult to refine prompts, since you have to preempt the chatbot's output and what it might overlook. At the same time, you have to make sure your prompts aren't unnecessarily long, since this also impacts usage limits.

I use ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini daily — here's the only one worth paying for

One stands above the rest.

16
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Hopefully Anthropic will find a way to provide a smoother experience

I'm hoping that the current restrictive limits on Claude will smooth out to provide a better experience for users. I feel like I've started using the tool at the worst possible time, even though I am impressed by its results.

For longtime users and those who pay a subscription, I imagine the change is even more frustrating. Claude is extremely promising — it would be a pity if it loses footing in the market due to people being unable to actually use it.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
Group pricing
$100/month per person for the Max plan

Claude is an AI assistant and LLM developed by Anthropic.

See at Claude

item.title: Windows 11 is finally getting a movable taskbar, and it only took Microsoft five years
item.itunes:title: Windows 11 is finally getting a movable taskbar, and it only took Microsoft five years
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For five years, Windows 11 users have lived in a world where the taskbar was treated like a wall, glued to the bottom of the screen. It was an irritating design choice that took away decades of muscle memory and forced power users into a sea of third-party workarounds like StartAllBack.

But the long nightmare is finally ending. Microsoft has officially announced that the taskbar is gaining the one feature it should have never lost: the ability to move. Now, Windows users everywhere can finally stop using third-party patches to get back a feature they have had since 1995.

7 design flaws in Windows 11 Microsoft still hasn't fixed

What are we waiting for?

15
By  João Carrasqueira

The five-year freeze is over

The 1825-day wait

Since Windows 11 dropped in 2021, power users have been asking for a movable taskbar on their desktops. We were told the taskbar had been rebuilt from the ground up (more on that in a minute), and the sides of our monitors simply didn’t exist.

Word from inside is that the movable taskbar has been bumped to Priority 0 (P0) status. In the world of software development, P0 isn’t just a to-do item; it’s a ‘drop everything’ directive.

It means the feature is now considered mission-critical, almost at a level of fixing a major security flaw or a system-breaking bug. Microsoft even teased a vertical taskbar recently and took down the video after it went viral.

Microsoft is finally sending a loud, clear signal: they have stopped trying to win arguments with their power users and have started trying to win back our trust. It’s a total 180-degree turn from the ‘take it or leave it’ attitude of 2021.

Why did the taskbar get stuck in the first place

A brief history

It turns out the reason the taskbar got stuck wasn’t a lack of imagination, but a combination of technical debt and a very specific, data-driven decision.

When Windows 11 was being built, Microsoft made the decision to toss out the legacy code that had powered the taskbar for decades. They rebuilt it from the ground up using modern code.

Microsoft had to cherry-pick which features to build first, and for a long time, the ability to move the taskbar didn’t make the cut.

One thing I learned from digging into Microsoft’s explanations is that moving the taskbar isn’t as simple as just flipping the switch. When the taskbar sits at the bottom, every app knows exactly how much horizontal and vertical space it has.

But the moment you move that bar to the left or right, the math breaks. Suddenly, apps have to re-flow their layouts, resize content, and handle snapping behavior in a different way. It was a huge engineering challenge for legacy Win32 apps and modern UWP apps.

Besides, their internal survey suggested that only a small percentage of people actually moved their taskbar, so they prioritized features like AI agents and touch gestures instead.

Thankfully, that era is ending. The move to P0 status proves that Microsoft has finally realized that low usage doesn’t mean low importance.

I finally set up ExplorerPatcher on Windows, and I should have sooner

Windows 11 is much better this way

5
By  João Carrasqueira

Beyond the taskbar

A leaner, faster Windows

I have spent a lot of time criticizing the odd design choices and web wrappers on Windows 11. However, looking at Microsoft’s recent announcements, the company isn’t just fixing the taskbar; they are trying to breathe new life into Windows 11.

It’s a strategic move to win back the silent majority of power users who have been eyeing Linux or macOS simply because they felt ignored.

I’m also optimistic about the quiet death of the web-wrappers on Windows. For a while, it felt like every new Windows feature was just a glorified website running in a heavy, RAM-hungry container. But future updates will pivot back to native performance.

Then there is the AI elephant in the room. We all saw the ‘AI everywhere’ approach, where Microsoft was throwing Copilot in every corner of the OS.

Instead of being a forced centerpiece, Copilot is being ‘right-sized.’ Microsoft plans to offer it in the workflows where it actually adds value. This is how Microsoft plans to win back everyone: the general users get a helpful assistant with snappy performance from core apps like File Explorer, and the power users get their screen real estate back.

The monitors have sides, too

After years of following minimalism, Windows finally seems to be remembering who its users actually are. By restoring the movable taskbar, Microsoft is signaling the flexibility that made Windows a powerhouse in the first place.

This shift, coupled with the recent streamlining of Copilot into a more focused tool and a commitment to high-performance native apps, suggests a major turning point for the OS.

If this is the new Microsoft — one that listens to feedback, focuses on speed and stability over AI gimmicks — then the next five years of Windows look brighter than the last.

item.title: Ubuntu 26.04 now needs more RAM than Windows 11, which says more about Microsoft than Canonical
item.itunes:title: Ubuntu 26.04 now needs more RAM than Windows 11, which says more about Microsoft than Canonical
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Ubuntu 26.04 LTS is due to release on April 23, supported through April 2031.
  • Its new system requirements include a 2GHz dual-core, 6GB RAM, and 25GB free disk space.
  • It's more than Windows 11's 4GB requirement, but Ubuntu lists a comfortable minimum; Windows' spec is the bare minimum.

Ubuntu 26.04 LTS's release date is on the horizon, with the operating system being released on 23rd April if everything goes well. As part of the lead-up to the release, the developers behind Ubuntu, Canonical, have updated the system requirements for the operating system.

Despite Ubuntu 26.04 LTS's new system specifications being quite light for a modern-day operating system, it has notably placed the RAM requirements above Windows 11's recommendation, the operating system that Linux users often call 'bloated'. However, before you wonder if the FOSS sky is falling, it's worth actually taking a look at what Microsoft thinks you'll be able to run Windows 11 off of.

7 signs it's finally time to upgrade your RAM

That 8GB or 16GB kit might be on its last legs

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Ubuntu 26.04 LTS asks for 6GB of RAM

It's more than Windows 11's 4GB requirement, but good luck running it with that

As spotted by Neowin, Canonical has released the Ubuntu 26.04 LTS documentation ahead of its release date. It details some good-to-know stats as to what to expect from the new release, such as how the OS will be supported all the way until April 2031. However, the documentation also includes a new batch of system requirements, and it includes an increase in RAM:

Ubuntu Desktop 26.04 LTS requires a 2 GHz dual-core processor or better, a minimum of 6GB RAM and 25 GB of free hard drive space.

While 6GB of RAM is still pretty light for an operating system, what's interesting is that this is 50% more RAM needed than what Microsoft claims you need for Windows 11. Not only that, but Microsoft says Windows 11 will be right at home on a 1 GHz dual-core processor.

So, is this the end of open-source operating systems being the lighter, less bloated option compared to Windows? Have some of the core tenets behind Linux distros finally fallen, spelling doom for us all? Well, not really. While it's true that Canonical has listed higher specifications than Windows 11, there's one problem: have you tried using Windows 11 on a 1 GHz dual-core processor and 4GB of RAM?

Running Windows 11 off 4GB of RAM is technically possible, much like how it's technically possible to play a video game running at 10FPS to completion. However, like the video game, it's not going to be a smooth or pleasant experience. As such, it's best to look at Canonical's system requirements as what you need for a comfortable experience, while Windows 11's is more the bare minimum. And you do not want to run your daily driver off the bare minimum.

CachyOS beats Windows 11 in Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 in a new gaming benchmark

Windows is no longer the king of gaming.

14
By  Simon Batt
item.description:
Summary

  • Ubuntu 26.04 LTS is due to release on April 23, supported through April 2031.
  • Its new system requirements include a 2GHz dual-core, 6GB RAM, and 25GB free disk space.
  • It's more than Windows 11's 4GB requirement, but Ubuntu lists a comfortable minimum; Windows' spec is the bare minimum.

Ubuntu 26.04 LTS's release date is on the horizon, with the operating system being released on 23rd April if everything goes well. As part of the lead-up to the release, the developers behind Ubuntu, Canonical, have updated the system requirements for the operating system.

Despite Ubuntu 26.04 LTS's new system specifications being quite light for a modern-day operating system, it has notably placed the RAM requirements above Windows 11's recommendation, the operating system that Linux users often call 'bloated'. However, before you wonder if the FOSS sky is falling, it's worth actually taking a look at what Microsoft thinks you'll be able to run Windows 11 off of.

7 signs it's finally time to upgrade your RAM

That 8GB or 16GB kit might be on its last legs

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Ubuntu 26.04 LTS asks for 6GB of RAM

It's more than Windows 11's 4GB requirement, but good luck running it with that

As spotted by Neowin, Canonical has released the Ubuntu 26.04 LTS documentation ahead of its release date. It details some good-to-know stats as to what to expect from the new release, such as how the OS will be supported all the way until April 2031. However, the documentation also includes a new batch of system requirements, and it includes an increase in RAM:

Ubuntu Desktop 26.04 LTS requires a 2 GHz dual-core processor or better, a minimum of 6GB RAM and 25 GB of free hard drive space.

While 6GB of RAM is still pretty light for an operating system, what's interesting is that this is 50% more RAM needed than what Microsoft claims you need for Windows 11. Not only that, but Microsoft says Windows 11 will be right at home on a 1 GHz dual-core processor.

So, is this the end of open-source operating systems being the lighter, less bloated option compared to Windows? Have some of the core tenets behind Linux distros finally fallen, spelling doom for us all? Well, not really. While it's true that Canonical has listed higher specifications than Windows 11, there's one problem: have you tried using Windows 11 on a 1 GHz dual-core processor and 4GB of RAM?

Running Windows 11 off 4GB of RAM is technically possible, much like how it's technically possible to play a video game running at 10FPS to completion. However, like the video game, it's not going to be a smooth or pleasant experience. As such, it's best to look at Canonical's system requirements as what you need for a comfortable experience, while Windows 11's is more the bare minimum. And you do not want to run your daily driver off the bare minimum.

CachyOS beats Windows 11 in Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 in a new gaming benchmark

Windows is no longer the king of gaming.

14
By  Simon Batt

item.title: Liquid metal works great as thermal paste, but the hidden costs aren't worth it
item.itunes:title: Liquid metal works great as thermal paste, but the hidden costs aren't worth it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Thermal interface materials seldom get a mention in the PC building discourse, but it's an essential catalyst for effective CPU and GPU cooling. Selecting the right thermal compound can often represent a thin line between frustrating thermal throttling that comes up as micro-stutters and seamless performance.

Liquid metal has been emerging as a high-performance alternative for many enthusiasts, particularly those into overclocking and pushing their silicon to the absolute limit. The appeal is certainly undeniable. Even the worst liquid metal compound offers much higher thermal conductivity than the best aftermarket aluminum or zinc-oxide-based TIM, and the data proves just that. However, there are a few things every enthusiast should know before they make the switch.

It will likely void your warranty

And the manufacturers will certainly know

One of the rare moments in the PC building where I point users to the fine print of their CPU manufacturer's warranty is when they are considering liquid metal as TIM, and it's worth it every time. Intel explicitly states that using any liquid metal paste on their boxed processors can void the warranty, and there are some far-reaching consequences of this.

Even if your processor fails for a completely unrelated reason down the line, you'd still be unlikely to successfully RMA it. Most liquid metal TIMs use gallium as their base compound, and while it is exactly what affords it the excellent thermal conductivity, it's also corrosive to copper, which is what the CPU integrated heat spreaders (IHS) are made of. This corrosion will likely wear out the serial numbers on the IHS and won't go unnoticed during an inspection. If you are intent on applying liquid metal TIM to the IHS regardless, it's still worth taking five minutes to verify your manufacturer's warranty first to avoid any unpleasant surprises.

It comes with an unforgiving application process, too

Hundreds of dollars of risk, for only a few degrees?

As someone who has a propensity to accidentally bake a cake on top of my IHS, I can say with confidence that imperfectly applying a conventional thermal compound is not even concerning. I can always clean it off with a micro-fibre cloth dipped in isopropyl alcohol, reapply, and move on. With liquid metal, things are a little more challenging.

Not only do you have to be extremely meticulous with the placement of the bulbous release of the compound, you also need to be extremely precise with the amount that you're using. Because gallium is electrically conductive, any excessive material that strays beyond the IHS can contact components on the surrounding PCB and short your motherboard, which, in this hardware economy, is a real pain to replace.

It absolutely does not travel well

It's best to avoid it if your build isn't staying put

Liquid metal can perform somewhat reliably if applied cautiously and in a build that stays in stationary environments. However, the moment mobility enters the equation, it introduces a couple of other risks. Unlike zinc and aluminum-oxide thermal paste, liquid metal is made out of gallium, which has an extremely low melting point of about 29.7°C. When applied incorrectly, it can shift with movement, and since portable devices such as laptops are exposed to regular travel, this can also cause the fluid to migrate.

Recently, renowned tech YouTuber Linus Sebastian damaged a laptop as a result of this liquid metal migration around the PCB due to an imperfect seal and rough handling. Although such an occurrence is rare, and it certainly forms more of an edge case, it only goes to show that even industry experts aren't immune to the risks of a bad application and a clumsy re-assembly. It's also worth noting that most laptop cooling assemblies use aluminum heatsinks (or an aluminum alloy), which gallium can erode just as easily as copper.

There are much safer ways to reduce your temps

Liquid metal has many different ways of being more harmful than beneficial for your PC, and if you're looking for a convenient way to reduce your PC's temperatures, there are ways to do so without needing to disassemble your rig or having to remove the back panel of your laptop. Undervolting your system can be a surprisingly effective way to bring the temperatures down without touching any hardware at all. On Intel systems, the XTU (Extreme Tuning Utility) can be a great starting point, while AMD users can use Ryzen Master to add custom curve optimizer offsets to do so. For laptop users who are either locked out of their BIOS or find undervolting far too complicated, a quality cooling pad is also worth considering as a passive but effective way to keep the temperatures in check.

If your thermal compound has been in use for more than a year or two, and you suspect that it's absolutely time for a re-pasting, a carbon-filler paste like the Arctic MX-6 can also be a high-performance (albeit, safer) alternative.

Arctic MX-6
Electrically conductive
No
Density
2.6 g/cm³

The Arctic MX-6 is the direct successor to the popular MX-5 thermal paste solution. It's promised to deliver an uplift of 20% in thermal performance with increased viscosity. If you want the best overall thermal solution for your CPU, this is it.

$10 at Amazon $11 at Newegg

item.title: I stopped manually organizing 90+ NotebookLM notebooks once I found these two underrated tools
item.itunes:title: I stopped manually organizing 90+ NotebookLM notebooks once I found these two underrated tools
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I’ve been using NotebookLM for long enough now to know its pain points, and the lack of organizational features is something I’ve just kind of accepted. I made do, tried to find workarounds, and just tolerated the chaos. Then I started getting closer to my notebook cap (I’m using the free tier), and it dawned on me that I was going to have to do some decluttering. And since NotebookLM doesn’t have many organizational functions, it would need to be done manually - painstakingly sifting through all the notebooks and every notebook’s contents to decide what’s worth keeping or tossing.

Since NotebookLM’s integrations with other tools are few and far between, I started thinking of any that I might already have access to but hadn’t really considered that could simplify the process. Turns out I was overlooking two pretty obvious ones - browser extensions and Gemini. Between them, they sort out pretty much everything I’ve been frustrated about…

NotebookLM’s biggest problem

Everything sits in one big pile

I can see what NotebookLM was going for with its super minimalist layout. I tend to appreciate apps that focus only on the basics and don’t overload you with features. But sometimes it feels like NotebookLM mistakes “simple” with “no tools whatsoever”, and at some point that stops being a design philosophy and starts being a problem. Of course, the problem I’m talking about is the lack of organization both in the dashboard and inside the notebooks.

I’ll give NotebookLM some credit for adding a couple of filtering options recently. You can now view your notebooks in a list or grid, and sort them by title or most recent. But that still doesn’t get you very far. There are no tags, no folders, no batch select, and no global search. And the problem gets worse when you’re inside a notebook - you can’t move around your sources, group them, or tag them either. And probably my biggest pain point: adding a new source will auto-select all of your sources, overriding any custom selection/deselection you had, so you have to go through the whole list again and remember which ones were selected/deselected.

If you’re using NotebookLM casually with a handful of notebooks, none of this is a dealbreaker. But if you’re a heavier user like me, it compounds. I’d rather create a new notebook than sort through an old one because of how inconvenient NotebookLM makes it, which also means I’m eating up my usage cap. NotebookLM rewards heavy use, but it also punishes it, albeit unintentionally.

NotebookLM is already great, but these 4 features would make it even better

Good? Yes. Perfect? Not yet.

5
By  Mahnoor Faisal

My secret weapon for whipping my notebooks into shape

All it takes is the right browser extension

If you’re a heavy NotebookLM user, you’ve probably already installed a couple of browser extensions that simplify the process of getting sources into a notebook. If you haven’t heard of NotebookLM Tools yet, I suggest getting it - it’s honestly the only NotebookLM extension you’ll ever need because it handles everything. NotebookLM Tools’ biggest appeal to me at first was the ability to add sources in bulk. It scans your open browser tabs, as well as embedded links within each of those web pages, and lets you add everything to a notebook in one swoop.

But its true power lies in its separate interface, which gives you all the organizational features NotebookLM won’t. In the dashboard, you’ll see a layout of all your notebooks, as well as options for individual notebooks and global filters and controls. To start my NotebookLM decluttering journey, I jumped right into the batch-selection function, selected every notebook that I knew was old, outdated, or meant to be temporary, and deleted them right away. The batch selection button is the little tickbox in the top left corner of every notebook.

The next step was renaming. NotebookLM Tools has a batch-rename feature which lets you rename multiple notebooks at once using a Replace/Add Text function. And then came the tagging, which is basically a stand-in for NotebookLM’s lack of folders. You can create as many tags as you want and assign them to multiple notebooks, and clicking on a tag will take you to a folder of notebooks with that tag. The really cool thing is that these tags will show up in NotebookLM as well. So if you click on a notebook’s tag in the NotebookLM dashboard, you’ll be directed to that tag’s folder in NotebookLM Tools.

Everything you add or change in NotebookLM Tools reflects in real-time in NotebookLM. And while I typically don’t appreciate tool-hopping, I’m more than fine hopping over to another tool if that means being able to organize my notebooks.

NotebookLM Tools
See at NotebookLM Tools

The most overlooked NotebookLM organization trick

It’s all thanks to Gemini’s integration

NotebookLM doesn’t have a lot of official integrations beyond Google’s other apps, but I didn’t want to bother with MCP server setups or CLIs. And turns out I didn’t need to, all I needed was Gemini. Google integrated NotebookLM with Gemini a while ago now, and it’s honestly one of the most underrated ways of “speaking” with your notebooks. Gemini can’t actually modify your notebooks as it only has read-only access, but that’s been more than enough for my use case.

I simply do this: In a fresh chat, I add a NotebookLM notebook (plus icon > NotebookLM > select notebook), and then I’ll prompt Gemini about the state of the notebook. Sure, I could just prompt NotebookLM about what’s in my notebook, but it doesn’t have outside context to understand what the notebook is actually about. For example, I could prompt Gemini “which sources veer too far off from ux design principles?” regarding my UX design notebook. It actually gave me a solid answer and pointed out sources that didn’t belong in that notebook.

You can get pretty specific and ask it to point out which sources are older, newer, have more context, less context, what topics they cover, and if their talking points are even relevant to the notebook, etc. Gemini will pull from its own data and web search to give you a clear overview of the state of your sources within that context. While you will need to go back into the notebook and get rid of the flagged sources manually, Gemini pretty much handles all the initial sorting for you.

Finally organizing my NotebookLM notebooks

Unfortunately, NotebookLM still requires workarounds for you to organize your notebooks. I went into this knowing I was going to have to set up at least one third-party tool and tool-hop, but NotebookLM Tools and Gemini made it way less tedious than I expected it to be. If you’re struggling with keeping your notebooks in shape, I recommend giving this combo a shot.

The most overlooked NotebookLM organization trick

It’s all thanks to Gemini’s integration

NotebookLM doesn’t have a lot of official integrations beyond Google’s other apps, but I didn’t want to bother with MCP server setups or CLIs. And turns out I didn’t need to, all I needed was Gemini. Google integrated NotebookLM with Gemini a while ago now, and it’s honestly one of the most underrated ways of “speaking” with your notebooks. Gemini can’t actually modify your notebooks as it only has read-only access, but that’s been more than enough for my use case.

I simply do this: In a fresh chat, I add a NotebookLM notebook (plus icon > NotebookLM > select notebook), and then I’ll prompt Gemini about the state of the notebook. Sure, I could just prompt NotebookLM about what’s in my notebook, but it doesn’t have outside context to understand what the notebook is actually about. For example, I could prompt Gemini “which sources veer too far off from ux design principles?” regarding my UX design notebook. It actually gave me a solid answer and pointed out sources that didn’t belong in that notebook.

You can get pretty specific and ask it to point out which sources are older, newer, have more context, less context, what topics they cover, and if their talking points are even relevant to the notebook, etc. Gemini will pull from its own data and web search to give you a clear overview of the state of your sources within that context. While you will need to go back into the notebook and get rid of the flagged sources manually, Gemini pretty much handles all the initial sorting for you.

Finally organizing my NotebookLM notebooks

Unfortunately, NotebookLM still requires workarounds for you to organize your notebooks. I went into this knowing I was going to have to set up at least one third-party tool and tool-hop, but NotebookLM Tools and Gemini made it way less tedious than I expected it to be. If you’re struggling with keeping your notebooks in shape, I recommend giving this combo a shot.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve been using NotebookLM for long enough now to know its pain points, and the lack of organizational features is something I’ve just kind of accepted. I made do, tried to find workarounds, and just tolerated the chaos. Then I started getting closer to my notebook cap (I’m using the free tier), and it dawned on me that I was going to have to do some decluttering. And since NotebookLM doesn’t have many organizational functions, it would need to be done manually - painstakingly sifting through all the notebooks and every notebook’s contents to decide what’s worth keeping or tossing.

Since NotebookLM’s integrations with other tools are few and far between, I started thinking of any that I might already have access to but hadn’t really considered that could simplify the process. Turns out I was overlooking two pretty obvious ones - browser extensions and Gemini. Between them, they sort out pretty much everything I’ve been frustrated about…

NotebookLM’s biggest problem

Everything sits in one big pile

I can see what NotebookLM was going for with its super minimalist layout. I tend to appreciate apps that focus only on the basics and don’t overload you with features. But sometimes it feels like NotebookLM mistakes “simple” with “no tools whatsoever”, and at some point that stops being a design philosophy and starts being a problem. Of course, the problem I’m talking about is the lack of organization both in the dashboard and inside the notebooks.

I’ll give NotebookLM some credit for adding a couple of filtering options recently. You can now view your notebooks in a list or grid, and sort them by title or most recent. But that still doesn’t get you very far. There are no tags, no folders, no batch select, and no global search. And the problem gets worse when you’re inside a notebook - you can’t move around your sources, group them, or tag them either. And probably my biggest pain point: adding a new source will auto-select all of your sources, overriding any custom selection/deselection you had, so you have to go through the whole list again and remember which ones were selected/deselected.

If you’re using NotebookLM casually with a handful of notebooks, none of this is a dealbreaker. But if you’re a heavier user like me, it compounds. I’d rather create a new notebook than sort through an old one because of how inconvenient NotebookLM makes it, which also means I’m eating up my usage cap. NotebookLM rewards heavy use, but it also punishes it, albeit unintentionally.

NotebookLM is already great, but these 4 features would make it even better

Good? Yes. Perfect? Not yet.

5
By  Mahnoor Faisal

My secret weapon for whipping my notebooks into shape

All it takes is the right browser extension

If you’re a heavy NotebookLM user, you’ve probably already installed a couple of browser extensions that simplify the process of getting sources into a notebook. If you haven’t heard of NotebookLM Tools yet, I suggest getting it - it’s honestly the only NotebookLM extension you’ll ever need because it handles everything. NotebookLM Tools’ biggest appeal to me at first was the ability to add sources in bulk. It scans your open browser tabs, as well as embedded links within each of those web pages, and lets you add everything to a notebook in one swoop.

But its true power lies in its separate interface, which gives you all the organizational features NotebookLM won’t. In the dashboard, you’ll see a layout of all your notebooks, as well as options for individual notebooks and global filters and controls. To start my NotebookLM decluttering journey, I jumped right into the batch-selection function, selected every notebook that I knew was old, outdated, or meant to be temporary, and deleted them right away. The batch selection button is the little tickbox in the top left corner of every notebook.

The next step was renaming. NotebookLM Tools has a batch-rename feature which lets you rename multiple notebooks at once using a Replace/Add Text function. And then came the tagging, which is basically a stand-in for NotebookLM’s lack of folders. You can create as many tags as you want and assign them to multiple notebooks, and clicking on a tag will take you to a folder of notebooks with that tag. The really cool thing is that these tags will show up in NotebookLM as well. So if you click on a notebook’s tag in the NotebookLM dashboard, you’ll be directed to that tag’s folder in NotebookLM Tools.

Everything you add or change in NotebookLM Tools reflects in real-time in NotebookLM. And while I typically don’t appreciate tool-hopping, I’m more than fine hopping over to another tool if that means being able to organize my notebooks.

NotebookLM Tools
See at NotebookLM Tools

The most overlooked NotebookLM organization trick

It’s all thanks to Gemini’s integration

NotebookLM doesn’t have a lot of official integrations beyond Google’s other apps, but I didn’t want to bother with MCP server setups or CLIs. And turns out I didn’t need to, all I needed was Gemini. Google integrated NotebookLM with Gemini a while ago now, and it’s honestly one of the most underrated ways of “speaking” with your notebooks. Gemini can’t actually modify your notebooks as it only has read-only access, but that’s been more than enough for my use case.

I simply do this: In a fresh chat, I add a NotebookLM notebook (plus icon > NotebookLM > select notebook), and then I’ll prompt Gemini about the state of the notebook. Sure, I could just prompt NotebookLM about what’s in my notebook, but it doesn’t have outside context to understand what the notebook is actually about. For example, I could prompt Gemini “which sources veer too far off from ux design principles?” regarding my UX design notebook. It actually gave me a solid answer and pointed out sources that didn’t belong in that notebook.

You can get pretty specific and ask it to point out which sources are older, newer, have more context, less context, what topics they cover, and if their talking points are even relevant to the notebook, etc. Gemini will pull from its own data and web search to give you a clear overview of the state of your sources within that context. While you will need to go back into the notebook and get rid of the flagged sources manually, Gemini pretty much handles all the initial sorting for you.

Finally organizing my NotebookLM notebooks

Unfortunately, NotebookLM still requires workarounds for you to organize your notebooks. I went into this knowing I was going to have to set up at least one third-party tool and tool-hop, but NotebookLM Tools and Gemini made it way less tedious than I expected it to be. If you’re struggling with keeping your notebooks in shape, I recommend giving this combo a shot.

item.title: 4 affordable streaming services that finally replaced Netflix for me
item.itunes:title: 4 affordable streaming services that finally replaced Netflix for me
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It feels like Netflix is no longer the one-stop shop. It's expensive, the library rotates too fast, and the Basic with Ads tier is a significant downgrade.

There's been a major shift recently, and the smartest streaming service users aren't paying $20 for a single service; they're stacking smaller, high-quality apps at half the price. With the crackdown on password sharing and prices going up yet again, it feels like many people are looking for the exit ramp.

There's a range of streaming apps on offer that provide specialized libraries or technical perks, like high bit rates or no-cost tiers, that Netflix completely lacks. These four apps offer better curation and specifications, or, in some cases, are entirely free, proving that the red just isn't the gold standard anymore.

I switched from streaming to self-hosting my media — it cost more than I expected

Trading a recurring cost for an upfront sticker price

38
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Mubi

The cinephile's dream

While Netflix uses algorithms to feed you content, Mubi uses humans to actually select movies. There's a rotating gallery of 30 handpicked films, so you don't spend 40 minutes scrolling to find something to watch, which leads to absolute streaming fatigue. You just press play, and you're good to go.

Mubi costs $15/month, but the streaming service frequently offers new subscribers three months for $1, as well as a 7-day free trial when this offer isn't in rotation. This means you can easily give Mubi a go and decide if it's for you without even paying a single penny.

Alongside this, you can opt for a $20/month Mubi Go plan, which includes a cinema ticket every week in select cities and the ability to stream any film on Mubi at any time. With four cinema trips in the bag, as well as access to Mubi's ever-changing roster of films, you're still paying less than the premium tier of Netflix.

Mubi
See at Google Play Store See at Apple App Store See at Mubi

Tubi

The free king

Credit: Tubi

Tubi is the best-kept secret in streaming. If you've yet to give it a go, it's a must-try for everyone because it's 100% free and owned by Fox. It's ad-supported, so you'll have to watch commercials occasionally, but it also means you don't have to pay anything to access its vast library of titles. The platform provides you with an unmatched deep-cut library that Netflix can't come close to touching. Do you want 90s cult horror films, niche documentaries, or obscure anime? Tubi has the stuff that Netflix deleted years ago to make room for shows like Is It Cake?

After a brief hiatus in several regions, Tubi officially relaunched in late 2024 with a massive library, and it's still completely free to use. If you're yet to give it a shot, then it's worth trying because it's entirely free. Even Netflix's basic tier, which also includes ads, costs you money, so Tubi feels like an absolutely fantastic alternative while still offering a vast library of titles.

Tubi
See at Google Play Store See at Apple App Store See at Tubi

Apple TV+

The premium option

Apple TV+ feels like the HBO of the modern era. In terms of quality-to-price, it's the king and takes home the crown pretty much every single time. It lets you adjust the bit rate, making it an absolute winner for those who care about streaming quality. Apple TV+ streams are significantly higher in bitrate than Netflix streams, which means 4K HDR content actually looks as it should, not a compressed, blocky mess like it is often on other streaming services.

On top of that, every show is a high-budget prestige play like Severance, Silo, or For All Mankind, and more. It's fully global and costs $10/month. If you buy an Apple product, you'll get three months free with your purchase. With this in mind, it's worth digging through your Apple devices to see if you already have access to a three-month trial, then giving Apple TV+ a shot.

Apple TV+
See at Google Play Store See at Apple App Store $10/mo

Kanopy

The educational powerhouse

Kanopy is a hidden library card hack. It provides you with high-quality streaming and is paid for by your taxes. So long as you have a library card, which is free in most places, you can access Kanopy for free. It's completely ad-free and features A24 films, Criterion Collection titles, and more. Anyone who has a participating public library card or a university login can access Kanopy.

Make smarter streaming choices—subscribe to our newsletter

Subscribe to the newsletter for clear coverage of smarter streaming choices: curated alternatives, side-by-side comparisons, and practical stacking ideas to replace bloated subscriptions. Plus broader media and tech coverage to inform your next streaming move.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

You need access to a valid library card number or university login, but once you've got that, you can stream films or documentaries and a whole host of other educational content.

Kanopy
See at Google Play Store See at Apple App Store See at Kanopy
6 essential free streaming apps every cord cutter needs

These six free apps give cord cutters a reason to close their wallet.

3
By  Marshall Gunnell

It might be time to ditch Netflix

While some people hate switching between multiple streaming services, the stacking strategy can help build a better bundle. You can get Tubi, Apple TV, and Mubi for less than the cost of a single Netflix Premium 4K subscription. Having this stack gives you a mix of free blockbusters, prestige sci-fi shows, and art house cinema without the Netflix bloat and the streaming fatigue that comes with it.

Years ago, Netflix's dominance relied on being the only player in town; however, the town is now full of better, cheaper options. It's easier to shop around with countless streaming services on offer. If you haven't watched anything on Netflix in three weeks, but you're still paying for it, it's probably time to cancel and try out a stack of better options.

If you buy an Apple product, you'll get three months free with your purchase. With this in mind, it's worth digging through your Apple devices to see if you already have access to a three-month trial, then giving Apple TV+ a shot.

Kanopy

The educational powerhouse

Kanopy is a hidden library card hack. It provides you with high-quality streaming and is paid for by your taxes. So long as you have a library card, which is free in most places, you can access Kanopy for free. It's completely ad-free and features A24 films, Criterion Collection titles, and more. Anyone who has a participating public library card or a university login can access Kanopy.

item.title: VS Code is slowly becoming an operating system, and I’m not sure that’s a good thing
item.itunes:title: VS Code is slowly becoming an operating system, and I’m not sure that’s a good thing
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Visual Studio Code is no longer just a text editor, a place to write code, preview Markdown, or maybe debug a script or two. Over the last few years, the app has quietly evolved from a popular code editor into something far bigger – an open source platform for building, extending, and even hosting entire development environments.

VS Code today includes a file explorer, terminal, extension marketplace, UI framework, remote access layer, and increasingly, AI tooling. This reduces friction and makes workflows easier to manage across machines and environments. But at the same time, it concentrates more control and responsibility in one platform, which introduces trade-offs that are easy to overlook when focusing only on convenience.

4 VS Code forks built for specific tasks

The classic VS Code is great and all, but these specialized forks are better for certain programming tasks

By  Ayush Pande

From editor to platform

VS Code is no longer just a code editor

VS Code’s shift from editor to platform happened gradually through a series of feature additions. Extensions were the first major step, turning the editor into something that could be reshaped based on what you need. Over time, more core capabilities began to rely on the same extension system, which blurred the line between built-in features and add-ons.

This design makes VS Code behave less like a standalone tool and more like a runtime environment. Many features that would traditionally exist as separate applications now operate within the editor using shared APIs. File handling is a good example of this shift. VS Code does not limit itself to local files. Through APIs like FileSystemProvider, extensions can expose entire virtual or remote file systems inside the workspace. That means a repository or remote environment can appear alongside local folders without requiring a complex setup.

Execution works in a similar way. The integrated terminal allows commands and scripts to run directly inside the editor, while extensions can create and manage their own terminals. This means build tools, scripts, and workflows are no longer external dependencies but part of the same environment.

The UI system pushes this platform idea even further. Extensions can build full interfaces using webviews with standard web tech, so you can run fairly complex tools right inside VS Code instead of opening separate apps. Notebook support moving into core APIs adds to this by making interactive workflows feel like a built-in part of the editor rather than something external.

Remote development takes this beyond your own machine. VS Code can connect to containers, remote servers, or subsystems, run extensions there, and still keep the interface local. Your workspace becomes portable, and the editor turns into the place you access different environments from.

The extension marketplace is what ties all of this together. It works like a distribution layer where you install, update, and manage tools in one place. Teams can lock this down with private marketplaces, which makes VS Code feel less like an editor and more like a controlled platform. Put together, all of this turns VS Code into a central layer where your tools, interfaces, and environments all come together.

The risks of consolidation shouldn’t be ignored

There are security risks as well

The benefits of consolidation are obvious, but they come with structural risks that matter more as VS Code takes on a bigger role. One of the biggest concerns is extension privilege. According to Microsoft’s own documentation, extensions run with the same permissions as VS Code, which means they can access files, make network requests, and execute processes. That puts them much closer to full applications than lightweight plugins.

Security research backs this up. Studies show that VS Code extensions aren’t sandboxed and can introduce real vulnerabilities, including code execution risks in widely used extensions. Many also depend on large dependency trees, which adds more supply chain exposure. There are safeguards like Workspace Trust, marketplace scanning, extension signing, and publisher verification prompts. These help reduce risk, but they don’t change the core model where extensions still run with broad permissions.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter VS Code platform insight

Subscribing to the newsletter unlocks deeper, actionable context on editor-as-platform trends, covering VS Code's platform growth, extension privilege and supply-chain risks, and marketplace control, so you can better evaluate the trade-offs shaping your tooling.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Another factor is ecosystem control. VS Code is one of the most widely used tools among developers, and at that scale, it effectively becomes the default, with tools and workflows increasingly built around it. At the same time, distribution is tied to Microsoft’s marketplace and licensing terms. The product license allows data collection and points to marketplace policies, while the marketplace defines how extensions can be distributed and used. These policies also give Microsoft the ability to modify or discontinue parts of the ecosystem.

Telemetry adds to this. VS Code collects usage data to improve features and guide development, and while some controls exist, not all data collection is fully optional. Extensions can also collect their own telemetry independently.

Historically, extensible editors like Emacs and Vim offered similar flexibility, but their ecosystems were far less centralized. VS Code, with its scale, marketplace-driven distribution, and vendor control, creates a different dynamic where the platform itself becomes the primary environment for development.

I self-hosted this VS Code fork so that I can access it in my browser, and I'll never go back

Code-Server is perfect for a centralized programming workstation

1
By  Ayush Pande
item.description:

The classic VS Code is great and all, but these specialized forks are better for certain programming tasks

By  Ayush Pande

VS Code’s shift from editor to platform happened gradually through a series of feature additions. Extensions were the first major step, turning the editor into something that could be reshaped based on what you need. Over time, more core capabilities began to rely on the same extension system, which blurred the line between built-in features and add-ons.

This design makes VS Code behave less like a standalone tool and more like a runtime environment. Many features that would traditionally exist as separate applications now operate within the editor using shared APIs. File handling is a good example of this shift. VS Code does not limit itself to local files. Through APIs like FileSystemProvider, extensions can expose entire virtual or remote file systems inside the workspace. That means a repository or remote environment can appear alongside local folders without requiring a complex setup.

Execution works in a similar way. The integrated terminal allows commands and scripts to run directly inside the editor, while extensions can create and manage their own terminals. This means build tools, scripts, and workflows are no longer external dependencies but part of the same environment.

The UI system pushes this platform idea even further. Extensions can build full interfaces using webviews with standard web tech, so you can run fairly complex tools right inside VS Code instead of opening separate apps. Notebook support moving into core APIs adds to this by making interactive workflows feel like a built-in part of the editor rather than something external.

Remote development takes this beyond your own machine. VS Code can connect to containers, remote servers, or subsystems, run extensions there, and still keep the interface local. Your workspace becomes portable, and the editor turns into the place you access different environments from.

The extension marketplace is what ties all of this together. It works like a distribution layer where you install, update, and manage tools in one place. Teams can lock this down with private marketplaces, which makes VS Code feel less like an editor and more like a controlled platform. Put together, all of this turns VS Code into a central layer where your tools, interfaces, and environments all come together.

The risks of consolidation shouldn’t be ignored

There are security risks as well

The benefits of consolidation are obvious, but they come with structural risks that matter more as VS Code takes on a bigger role. One of the biggest concerns is extension privilege. According to Microsoft’s own documentation, extensions run with the same permissions as VS Code, which means they can access files, make network requests, and execute processes. That puts them much closer to full applications than lightweight plugins.

Security research backs this up. Studies show that VS Code extensions aren’t sandboxed and can introduce real vulnerabilities, including code execution risks in widely used extensions. Many also depend on large dependency trees, which adds more supply chain exposure. There are safeguards like Workspace Trust, marketplace scanning, extension signing, and publisher verification prompts. These help reduce risk, but they don’t change the core model where extensions still run with broad permissions.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter VS Code platform insight

Subscribing to the newsletter unlocks deeper, actionable context on editor-as-platform trends, covering VS Code's platform growth, extension privilege and supply-chain risks, and marketplace control, so you can better evaluate the trade-offs shaping your tooling.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Another factor is ecosystem control. VS Code is one of the most widely used tools among developers, and at that scale, it effectively becomes the default, with tools and workflows increasingly built around it. At the same time, distribution is tied to Microsoft’s marketplace and licensing terms. The product license allows data collection and points to marketplace policies, while the marketplace defines how extensions can be distributed and used. These policies also give Microsoft the ability to modify or discontinue parts of the ecosystem.

Telemetry adds to this. VS Code collects usage data to improve features and guide development, and while some controls exist, not all data collection is fully optional. Extensions can also collect their own telemetry independently.

Historically, extensible editors like Emacs and Vim offered similar flexibility, but their ecosystems were far less centralized. VS Code, with its scale, marketplace-driven distribution, and vendor control, creates a different dynamic where the platform itself becomes the primary environment for development.

I self-hosted this VS Code fork so that I can access it in my browser, and I'll never go back

Code-Server is perfect for a centralized programming workstation

1
By  Ayush Pande
item.content:encoded:
Visual Studio Code is no longer just a text editor, a place to write code, preview Markdown, or maybe debug a script or two. Over the last few years, the app has quietly evolved from a popular code editor into something far bigger – an open source platform for building, extending, and even hosting entire development environments.

VS Code today includes a file explorer, terminal, extension marketplace, UI framework, remote access layer, and increasingly, AI tooling. This reduces friction and makes workflows easier to manage across machines and environments. But at the same time, it concentrates more control and responsibility in one platform, which introduces trade-offs that are easy to overlook when focusing only on convenience.

4 VS Code forks built for specific tasks

The classic VS Code is great and all, but these specialized forks are better for certain programming tasks

By  Ayush Pande

From editor to platform

VS Code is no longer just a code editor

VS Code’s shift from editor to platform happened gradually through a series of feature additions. Many features that would traditionally exist as separate applications now operate within the editor using shared APIs. File handling is a good example of this shift. VS Code does not limit itself to local files. Through APIs like FileSystemProvider, extensions can expose entire virtual or remote file systems inside the workspace. That means a repository or remote environment can appear alongside local folders without requiring a complex setup.

Execution works in a similar way. The integrated terminal allows commands and scripts to run directly inside the editor, while extensions can create and manage their own terminals. This means build tools, scripts, and workflows are no longer external dependencies but part of the same environment.

The UI system pushes this platform idea even further. Extensions can build full interfaces using webviews with standard web tech, so you can run fairly complex tools right inside VS Code instead of opening separate apps. That puts them much closer to full applications than lightweight plugins.

Many also depend on large dependency trees, which adds more supply chain exposure. There are safeguards like Workspace Trust, marketplace scanning, extension signing, and publisher verification prompts. These help reduce risk, but they don’t change the core model where extensions still run with broad permissions.

Another factor is ecosystem control. VS Code is one of the most widely used tools among developers, and at that scale, it effectively becomes the default, with tools and workflows increasingly built around it. At the same time, distribution is tied to Microsoft’s marketplace and licensing terms. The product license allows data collection and points to marketplace policies, while the marketplace defines how extensions can be distributed and used. These policies also give Microsoft the ability to modify or discontinue parts of the ecosystem.

Telemetry adds to this. VS Code collects usage data to improve features and guide development, and while some controls exist, not all data collection is fully optional. Extensions can also collect their own telemetry independently.

Historically, extensible editors like Emacs and Vim offered similar flexibility, but their ecosystems were far less centralized. VS Code, with its scale, marketplace-driven distribution, and vendor control, creates a different dynamic where the platform itself becomes the primary environment for development.

I self-hosted this VS Code fork so that I can access it in my browser, and I'll never go back

Code-Server is perfect for a centralized programming workstation

1
By  Ayush Pande

item.title: Claude and NotebookLM proved that AI research isn't the gimmick I thought it was
item.itunes:title: Claude and NotebookLM proved that AI research isn't the gimmick I thought it was
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
A couple of days ago, I asked someone how they research things. The answer came almost instantly: "I just ask ChatGPT." Without really being able to control myself, I rolled my eyes (but not for the reason I would have a year ago). Back then, it would have been the "AI" part that really got me. The entire premise of trusting AI with something as important as research felt reckless. This time, though, it was the ChatGPT bit.

I simply couldn't grasp why someone would still rely exclusively on ChatGPT and not even explore what else is out there. Two of the tools that finally made me believe that AI research isn't just a gimmick are Google's NotebookLM and Anthropic's Claude, especially when you use the two together.

NotebookLM works with what you already have

Helping you deal with information overload

I've always thought that finding information is the easiest part of research. The difficult part is frankly everything that comes after. This includes actually making sense of your findings and comprehending them, seeing how one source supports or contradicts another, pulling out patterns across all your sources, synthesizing your sources, and simply working with what you already have. The biggest issue we currently struggle with is information overload. AI slop certainly plays into this, but there's simply too much information to process.

From the first time I heard the term "AI-powered research," my mental image of it was tools that help us actually make sense of the stuff we already have and cut through all the noise. This is exactly the problem NotebookLM was built to solve. The tool is powered by Gemini under the hood, but it essentially builds a self-contained, grounded knowledge base from whatever sources you feed it. These sources can be PDFs, research articles, YouTube videos, Word documents, webpages, eBooks, etc. Every knowledge base you create, which in NotebookLM's terms is called a notebook, knows your sources and your sources only.

Until NotebookLM, I never believed AI could be this game-changing for productivity

It transformed my view of AI, for the better.

7
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Once your sources are uploaded, you're greeted with an interface that's largely similar to your traditional AI chatbot with a chatbox and a conversation thread. The similarities end there. Every time you ask NotebookLM a question, the response it gives you is grounded right within your uploaded sources. This means that hallucinations are considerably reduced, and you don't need to worry as much about it confidently making things up. Of course, though, you can't just blindly trust the output the tool gives you. This is why every claim comes with inline citations that link directly back to where NotebookLM picked up the information from.

Beyond just interacting with your own sources by asking questions in natural language and getting fully-cited answers, NotebookLM also has a range of Studio outputs that have genuinely transformed the way I approach research. With a single click, you can generate reports, Slide Decks, Infographics, Data Sheets, Video Overviews, and NotebookLM's most viral feature yet — Audio Overviews. The Mind Maps feature is specifically excellent for finding connections across your sources.

The reason I mention these features here is because they give you the opportunity to see your sources from a different angle. That's exactly what a huge part of research is. Before NotebookLM, you'd need to do all of this manually. You'd need to revisit the same information until you see something you didn't see before.

Claude makes for an excellent thinking partner

It doesn't constantly praise you

You have your sources — you've already done that part of research. NotebookLM will certainly help you with actually understanding them, but at some point, you need to move beyond understanding what the sources say and start figuring out what you actually think about all of it. You need to form an argument, identify what's missing, and turn it into something coherent. That's something you need to do. It isn't something you can outsource to AI, but I think there's a lot of value in using it to think with you.

Rather than AI reading your sources and giving its thoughts on them, you can use it as a surface to think against. Claude is so far the most conversational chatbot I've used, and it's great at exactly the type of back-and-forth I'm talking about. You can bounce between half-formed ideas, ask it to criticize your argument, explore angles you haven't considered yet, and just talk conversationally through them.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter AI research

Explore deeper AI research coverage—subscribe to the newsletter for practical breakdowns of tools like NotebookLM and Claude, hands-on workflow tactics, source-grounding tips, and immediately actionable guidance you can apply to your own projects.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The reason why I don't recommend ChatGPT, Gemini, or even NotebookLM for this is because Claude is one of the few tools that has a bigger tendency of pushing back on you. If it doesn't agree with you, it won't glaze you and keep telling you that you're doing great. It'll actually push back and tell you where your reasoning falls short.

When you're forming an argument, the last thing you need is someone or something just validating every single thing you say. Of course, Claude can also bring in fresh perspectives beyond what's in your sources. So, the way NotebookLM and Claude work are practically inverse!

It all comes down to how you actually use these tools

AI tools are here. They were here yesterday, and they'll be here tomorrow. You can either keep ignoring them while others don't, or find ways to meaningfully integrate them into your workflow. NotebookLM and Claude are the two tools that genuinely showed me what meaningful integration actually looks like.
