item.title: I moved everything to Proxmox and learned that fewer boxes doesn't mean more reliable
item.itunes:title: I moved everything to Proxmox and learned that fewer boxes doesn't mean more reliable
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I didn’t move half of my home lab into Proxmox because I wanted to centralize everything for neatness. I did it because I got tired of little boxes scattered around my apartment, each doing one job and each becoming its own maintenance chore. A dedicated system for this, a Raspberry Pi for that, a mini PC doing something else entirely. It worked, but it also felt like I was carrying around a growing pile of tiny obligations.

The trade-off wasn’t really about performance, power draw, or even complexity.

Proxmox looked like the obvious answer. I could consolidate services, manage them from one place, take snapshots before I broke something, and stop wondering which device was running what. In many ways, that promise held up beautifully. What surprised me was that the trade-off wasn’t really about performance, power draw, or even complexity. It was about how differently a home lab feels when flexibility replaces physical separation.

I moved away from Unraid, and this is the only thing I miss

Sometimes, power comes at the cost of peace

3
By  Karandeep Singh

Consolidating services changed how I thought about reliability

Fewer boxes meant less clutter but more shared responsibility

The first thing Proxmox gave me was relief. I no longer had to keep track of a half-dozen little systems with their own updates, storage quirks, power adapters, and weird one-off behaviors. Spinning up an LXC for one service and a VM for another felt cleaner than maintaining separate hardware for each role. Even the mental load dropped, because the dashboard gave me a much better sense of what was running and what needed attention.

That kind of consolidation also made experimentation easier. I could try a new self-hosted app without dedicating an entire machine to it, then scrap it later without feeling like I had wasted hardware. Backups became more consistent, and snapshots gave me the confidence to make changes I might have avoided before. When everything lives in one environment, trying something new no longer feels like a whole weekend project.

But consolidation subtly changes the meaning of reliability. Before, if one Raspberry Pi died or one small server went sideways, only one service usually went down with it. Once several services share a single Proxmox host, the blast radius gets much bigger. A single maintenance reboot, storage issue, or host failure can take out multiple things at once. That doesn’t make the setup bad, but it does mean “fewer devices” and “more resilient” are not automatically the same thing.

Virtualizing everything makes growth almost dangerously easy

Convenience turns expansion into a habit before you notice

One of the most intoxicating things about Proxmox is how quickly it turns ideas into running services. You start with a few practical migrations, maybe a DNS tool, a dashboard, or a backup utility. Then you notice how easy it is to clone a container, test another app, or stand up a VM for a project you might or might not stick with. Before long, your clean consolidation project has quietly become a platform for endless expansion.

That sounds like a good problem to have, and sometimes it is. A home lab should be fun, and Proxmox does a great job of making infrastructure feel accessible rather than intimidating. It lowers the friction between curiosity and deployment in a way that standalone hardware often doesn’t. You can learn more, test more, and recover from mistakes more gracefully, which is a big reason so many people fall hard for it.

The catch is that easy growth can also blur your priorities. When it’s trivial to spin up something new, it becomes harder to ask whether that thing deserves to exist in your environment at all. You end up accumulating services the same way people accumulate browser tabs, each one useful in theory and slightly neglected in practice. Proxmox didn’t just help me replace hardware. It made me more willing to create a larger, busier lab than I would have tolerated with physical devices.

Dedicated hardware still solves problems that virtualization cannot

Some jobs stay better when they live elsewhere

This is the part where the old approach deserves some credit. A dedicated box with one job is often easier to understand at a glance, easier to troubleshoot in a panic, and easier to leave alone once it works. There’s a kind of honesty to that setup that virtualization can obscure. When your DNS server is on a tiny box, and your media server is on another, the boundaries are obvious, and the failure domains are too.

Dedicated hardware can also be better for services that benefit from physical separation or direct hardware access. Maybe it’s a router, maybe it’s something tied to USB devices, maybe it’s a low-power appliance that should just keep doing its one boring task forever. Those roles don’t always become better just because you can virtualize them. In some cases, moving them into Proxmox adds a layer of abstraction without adding enough value to justify it.

There’s also a practical lifestyle angle that matters more than enthusiasts sometimes admit. A home lab isn’t a data center, and most of us are building one in a bedroom, office, closet, or apartment corner. A dedicated box can be reassuring because it behaves like an appliance rather than an ongoing project. Proxmox can absolutely be stable, but it still encourages a tinkerer mindset, and not every service benefits from being part of that world.

The trade-off is worth it because the control is real

Central management still beats scattered systems for everyday use

Even with all of that, I’m still glad I made the switch. The biggest reason is simple: Proxmox gives me more control over my environment than a pile of disconnected systems ever did. I can see resource usage, manage backups more coherently, and move from idea to deployment without reorganizing my whole setup. For day-to-day administration, that level of visibility is hard to give up once you’ve had it.

It also made my home lab feel more intentional. Instead of every service arriving on its own piece of hardware with its own little story, I now have a platform with room for structure. I can decide what deserves a full VM, what belongs in a container, and what should stay outside the cluster entirely. That kind of flexibility is useful, but the real value is that it makes trade-offs visible instead of accidental.

That’s why I don’t think the lesson here is “virtualize everything” or “keep everything dedicated.” It’s that Proxmox changes the nature of your home lab, and you should walk into that with your eyes open. You gain convenience, visibility, and flexibility, but you also create stronger dependencies on shared infrastructure. Replacing half my home lab with Proxmox absolutely improved it, but it also forced me to be more deliberate about what should stay centralized and what should stay separate.

Not everything belongs in the same box

One of the easiest mistakes to make with Proxmox is assuming every service should move into it just because it can. In practice, the best candidates for virtualization are usually the ones that benefit from snapshots, quick recovery, and easy scaling, while core infrastructure and hardware-dependent services often deserve their own dedicated space. That split doesn’t make a home lab less elegant. It makes it more resilient.

Service type

Better on standalone hardware

Better virtualized in Proxmox

Why

Router / firewall

Yes

Sometimes

Network edge services are easier to trust on dedicated hardware, especially when the rest of the lab depends on them staying up.

DNS / ad blocking

Often

Yes, with caveats

Pi-hole or AdGuard Home work well virtualized, but a separate box can be smarter if you want DNS available during host maintenance.

Media server

Sometimes

Yes

Jellyfin or Plex often benefit from virtualization for backup and management, though passthrough and transcoding can make dedicated hardware more appealing.

Home automation

Sometimes

Yes, with caveats

Home Assistant works great in a VM, but USB radios and critical automations can make a dedicated system feel safer.

Dashboards, utilities, and test services

No

Yes

Uptime Kuma, dashboards, lightweight containers, and experimental apps are usually the clearest win for Proxmox.

Why this shift changed my home lab for good

What I expected from Proxmox was consolidation. What I got was a different relationship with my own infrastructure. The hardware footprint got smaller, but the environment itself became more interconnected, more flexible, and a little more demanding of good decisions. That was the real trade-off, and it took me longer than I expected to see it clearly.

The smartest move isn’t replacing everything, it’s knowing which parts of your setup benefit from being part of the same machine and which ones are better left alone.

I still think moving a big chunk of a home lab into Proxmox is one of the best upgrades an enthusiast can make. It makes experimentation easier, management cleaner, and backups far less chaotic than they tend to be on scattered, standalone systems. But it also rewards restraint in a way people don’t always mention. The smartest move isn’t replacing everything, it’s knowing which parts of your setup benefit from being part of the same machine and which ones are better left alone.

Proxmox

Proxmox is powerful, and with great power comes great responsibility.

See at Proxmox
item.description:
I didn’t move half of my home lab into Proxmox because I wanted to centralize everything for neatness. I did it because I got tired of little boxes scattered around my apartment, each doing one job and each becoming its own maintenance chore. A dedicated system for this, a Raspberry Pi for that, a mini PC doing something else entirely. It worked, but it also felt like I was carrying around a growing pile of tiny obligations.

The trade-off wasn’t really about performance, power draw, or even complexity.

Proxmox looked like the obvious answer. I could consolidate services, manage them from one place, take snapshots before I broke something, and stop wondering which device was running what. In many ways, that promise held up beautifully. What surprised me was that the trade-off wasn’t really about performance, power draw, or even complexity. It was about how differently a home lab feels when flexibility replaces physical separation.

I moved away from Unraid, and this is the only thing I miss

Sometimes, power comes at the cost of peace

3
By  Karandeep Singh

Consolidating services changed how I thought about reliability

Fewer boxes meant less clutter but more shared responsibility

The first thing Proxmox gave me was relief. I no longer had to keep track of a half-dozen little systems with their own updates, storage quirks, power adapters, and weird one-off behaviors. Spinning up an LXC for one service and a VM for another felt cleaner than maintaining separate hardware for each role. Even the mental load dropped, because the dashboard gave me a much better sense of what was running and what needed attention.

That kind of consolidation also made experimentation easier. I could try a new self-hosted app without dedicating an entire machine to it, then scrap it later without feeling like I had wasted hardware. Backups became more consistent, and snapshots gave me the confidence to make changes I might have avoided before. When everything lives in one environment, trying something new no longer feels like a whole weekend project.

But consolidation subtly changes the meaning of reliability. Before, if one Raspberry Pi died or one small server went sideways, only one service usually went down with it. Once several services share a single Proxmox host, the blast radius gets much bigger. A single maintenance reboot, storage issue, or host failure can take out multiple things at once. That doesn’t make the setup bad, but it does mean “fewer devices” and “more resilient” are not automatically the same thing.

Virtualizing everything makes growth almost dangerously easy

Convenience turns expansion into a habit before you notice

One of the most intoxicating things about Proxmox is how quickly it turns ideas into running services. You start with a few practical migrations, maybe a DNS tool, a dashboard, or a backup utility. Then you notice how easy it is to clone a container, test another app, or stand up a VM for a project you might or might not stick with. Before long, your clean consolidation project has quietly become a platform for endless expansion.

That sounds like a good problem to have, and sometimes it is. A home lab should be fun, and Proxmox does a great job of making infrastructure feel accessible rather than intimidating. It lowers the friction between curiosity and deployment in a way that standalone hardware often doesn’t. You can learn more, test more, and recover from mistakes more gracefully, which is a big reason so many people fall hard for it.

The catch is that easy growth can also blur your priorities. When it’s trivial to spin up something new, it becomes harder to ask whether that thing deserves to exist in your environment at all. You end up accumulating services the same way people accumulate browser tabs, each one useful in theory and slightly neglected in practice. Proxmox didn’t just help me replace hardware. It made me more willing to create a larger, busier lab than I would have tolerated with physical devices.

Dedicated hardware still solves problems that virtualization cannot

Some jobs stay better when they live elsewhere

This is the part where the old approach deserves some credit. A dedicated box with one job is often easier to understand at a glance, easier to troubleshoot in a panic, and easier to leave alone once it works. There’s a kind of honesty to that setup that virtualization can obscure. When your DNS server is on a tiny box, and your media server is on another, the boundaries are obvious, and the failure domains are too.

Dedicated hardware can also be better for services that benefit from physical separation or direct hardware access. Maybe it’s a router, maybe it’s something tied to USB devices, maybe it’s a low-power appliance that should just keep doing its one boring task forever. Those roles don’t always become better just because you can virtualize them. In some cases, moving them into Proxmox adds a layer of abstraction without adding enough value to justify it.

There’s also a practical lifestyle angle that matters more than enthusiasts sometimes admit. A home lab isn’t a data center, and most of us are building one in a bedroom, office, closet, or apartment corner. A dedicated box can be reassuring because it behaves like an appliance rather than an ongoing project. Proxmox can absolutely be stable, but it still encourages a tinkerer mindset, and not every service benefits from being part of that world.

The trade-off is worth it because the control is real

Central management still beats scattered systems for everyday use

Even with all of that, I’m still glad I made the switch. The biggest reason is simple: Proxmox gives me more control over my environment than a pile of disconnected systems ever did. I can see resource usage, manage backups more coherently, and move from idea to deployment without reorganizing my whole setup. For day-to-day administration, that level of visibility is hard to give up once you’ve had it.

It also made my home lab feel more intentional. Instead of every service arriving on its own piece of hardware with its own little story, I now have a platform with room for structure. I can decide what deserves a full VM, what belongs in a container, and what should stay outside the cluster entirely. That kind of flexibility is useful, but the real value is that it makes trade-offs visible instead of accidental.

That’s why I don’t think the lesson here is “virtualize everything” or “keep everything dedicated.” It’s that Proxmox changes the nature of your home lab, and you should walk into that with your eyes open. You gain convenience, visibility, and flexibility, but you also create stronger dependencies on shared infrastructure. Replacing half my home lab with Proxmox absolutely improved it, but it also forced me to be more deliberate about what should stay centralized and what should stay separate.

Not everything belongs in the same box

One of the easiest mistakes to make with Proxmox is assuming every service should move into it just because it can. In practice, the best candidates for virtualization are usually the ones that benefit from snapshots, quick recovery, and easy scaling, while core infrastructure and hardware-dependent services often deserve their own dedicated space. That split doesn’t make a home lab less elegant. It makes it more resilient.

Service type

Better on standalone hardware

Better virtualized in Proxmox

Why

Router / firewall

Yes

Sometimes

Network edge services are easier to trust on dedicated hardware, especially when the rest of the lab depends on them staying up.

DNS / ad blocking

Often

Yes, with caveats

Pi-hole or AdGuard Home work well virtualized, but a separate box can be smarter if you want DNS available during host maintenance.

Media server

Sometimes

Yes

Jellyfin or Plex often benefit from virtualization for backup and management, though passthrough and transcoding can make dedicated hardware more appealing.

Home automation

Sometimes

Yes, with caveats

Home Assistant works great in a VM, but USB radios and critical automations can make a dedicated system feel safer.

Dashboards, utilities, and test services

No

Yes

Uptime Kuma, dashboards, lightweight containers, and experimental apps are usually the clearest win for Proxmox.

Why this shift changed my home lab for good

What I expected from Proxmox was consolidation. What I got was a different relationship with my own infrastructure. The hardware footprint got smaller, but the environment itself became more interconnected, more flexible, and a little more demanding of good decisions. That was the real trade-off, and it took me longer than I expected to see it clearly.

The smartest move isn’t replacing everything, it’s knowing which parts of your setup benefit from being part of the same machine and which ones are better left alone.

I still think moving a big chunk of a home lab into Proxmox is one of the best upgrades an enthusiast can make. It makes experimentation easier, management cleaner, and backups far less chaotic than they tend to be on scattered, standalone systems. But it also rewards restraint in a way people don’t always mention. The smartest move isn’t replacing everything, it’s knowing which parts of your setup benefit from being part of the same machine and which ones are better left alone.

Proxmox

Proxmox is powerful, and with great power comes great responsibility.

See at Proxmox

item.title: Microsoft quietly buried 'for entertainment purposes only' in Copilot's Terms of Use
item.itunes:title: Microsoft quietly buried 'for entertainment purposes only' in Copilot's Terms of Use
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Copilot is marketed as a trusty assistant, yet its Terms warn that it can make mistakes.
  • Microsoft calls Copilot "for entertainment purposes only" in its Terms of Use.
  • Despite product hype, always double-check Copilot outputs; don't rely on it for important advice.

It feels like AI companies have two contradictory masks they try to wear at the same time. The first is the one you see in the advertisements, heralding the AI assistant as a knowledge powerhouse, a dependable workmate, and suitable for handling your everyday tasks. The second is buried deep within the legal documents, which state that the AI can lie, the AI can hallucinate, and you should always double-check what it says, no matter how confident it sounds.

Well, it turns out that Microsoft is no different. For years now, it has marketed Copilot as the perfect personal assistant, helping you get stuff done with Cowork, remembering what you did on your OS last week with Recall, and integrating the AI into all of its office apps (to the point where its productivity suite is called 'Microsoft 365 Copilot'). However, tucked away within its Terms of Use is a statement that says Copilot is for "entertainment purposes only" and you shouldn't use it for "important advice."

Microsoft wants to sync your passwords with Copilot, and I’m not sure how I feel about it

It won't see your passwords, but I'm still unsure

4
By  Simon Batt

Copilot is "for entertainment purposes only," claims its own Terms of Use

A stark contrast to how it's advertised

As spotted by Tom's Hardware, you can see the claim for yourself over on the Microsoft Copilot Terms of Use. For the most part, the Terms of Use covers the basics, such as how you shouldn't misuse the AI and how Microsoft can cut you off if it suspects you're not playing by the rules. However, under the section titled "IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES & WARNINGS," in bold, is this nugget:

Copilot is for entertainment purposes only. It can make mistakes, and it may not work as intended. Don’t rely on Copilot for important advice. Use Copilot at your own risk.

Given how Microsoft positions Copilot as a powerful co-worker that you can entrust to help you get work done, seeing it referred to as 'for entertainment purposes only' is a stark reminder of how much these companies feel they need to cover themselves from the potential damage their AIs can do. But as long as companies need to market their LLMs to the public, their two masks will stay firmly in place.

LG will finally let you remove the AI TV feature nobody asked for

It's here to stay

4
By  Simon Batt
